introSection: Colossus is a steel roller coaster located within the Lost City themed area at Thorpe Park in Chertsey, Surrey, England, UK. When it opened on 22 March 2002, Colossus was the first roller coaster with ten inversions and the first major roller coaster at the park. An exact replica, called 10 Inversion Roller Coaster, was later built at Chimelong Paradise in China in 2006 and several more 10-Inversion coasters have since been built with a revised layout. Colossus and 10 Inversion Roller Coaster held the record until May 2013, when The Smiler opened at Alton Towers with fourteen inversions.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The initial plan for Colossus was a launched Bolliger & Mabillard Sitting Coaster, much like The Incredible Hulk Coaster at Universal's Islands of Adventure. This was eventually cancelled in favour of a multi-inversion roller coaster from Intamin. The ride was announced in August 2001. During planning, the ride was known as Project Odyssey. The final piece of track was installed on 8 November 2001.

Colossus opened on 22 March 2002.

After the 2022 season, some sections of track were removed.

The ride was repainted during the 2023-2024 off-season.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The ride's design was adapted from that of Monte Makaya in Brazil, opened in 1998. The height was reduced by but the same set of inversions were used. Two extra barrel rolls were added, one at the end of original triple barrel roll (creating a quadruple barrel roll) and another just before the final brake run. Monte Makaya's helix was also removed.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: Riders are secured by over-the-shoulder harnesses. Colossus was the first roller coaster to use Intamin's second generation Multi-Looping trains, which are more streamlined and exposed.

fullText:
Colossus is a steel roller coaster located within the Lost City themed area at Thorpe Park in Chertsey, Surrey, England, UK. When it opened on 22 March 2002, Colossus was the first roller coaster with ten inversions and the first major roller coaster at the park. An exact replica, called 10 Inversion Roller Coaster, was later built at Chimelong Paradise in China in 2006 and several more 10-Inversion coasters have since been built with a revised layout. Colossus and 10 Inversion Roller Coaster held the record until May 2013, when The Smiler opened at Alton Towers with fourteen inversions.

The initial plan for Colossus was a launched Bolliger & Mabillard Sitting Coaster, much like The Incredible Hulk Coaster at Universal's Islands of Adventure. This was eventually cancelled in favour of a multi-inversion roller coaster from Intamin. The ride was announced in August 2001. During planning, the ride was known as Project Odyssey. The final piece of track was installed on 8 November 2001.

Colossus opened on 22 March 2002.

After the 2022 season, some sections of track were removed.

The ride was repainted during the 2023-2024 off-season.

The ride's design was adapted from that of Monte Makaya in Brazil, opened in 1998. The height was reduced by but the same set of inversions were used. Two extra barrel rolls were added, one at the end of original triple barrel roll (creating a quadruple barrel roll) and another just before the final brake run. Monte Makaya's helix was also removed.

Riders are secured by over-the-shoulder harnesses. Colossus was the first roller coaster to use Intamin's second generation Multi-Looping trains, which are more streamlined and exposed.
